text: It's never been easier to build an AI SAS. This is thanks to all the amazing technology we have readily available today. However, if you're just getting started, even figuring out what tools to use can be a challenge. In this video, I'll share the tech stack that I'm using to ship AI products. These days, it's not lack of tools that hold AI founders back. It's being overwhelmed by them. Every month there seems to be yet another tool, technique, or model that captures the tech news cycle. But here's what I've learned. The most successful founders aren't the ones using the latest shiny tool. They're the ones that build with what they know and ship faster than 99% of developers. Here, I'll share what that looks like for me as someone transitioning from data science to solo product development. My goal isn't to say that this is the best tech stack to use, but rather just simply share what's worked for me in case it's helpful for those coming from a similar background. Let's start with the programming language. My biggest obstacle in building my first AI application was that I had no experience with the standard web development tools. In other words, HTML, CSS, JavaScript, TypeScript, no coding experience in them whatsoever. I only knew Python. And so this gave me two options. One, I could spend months learning some subset of these tools. Or two, just use Python in keeping with advice from supreme soloreneur Peter Levels. I think you should build with the technology that you know. Use the language that you already know and start coding with that and see how far you can get. The text tech, it's not the most important of a business when you're starting out a business. The important thing is that you validate that there's a market, that there's a product that people want to pay for. So use whatever you can use. So with that, I went with the ladder. The main tool that allowed me to do this was fast HTML. This is a Python library that allows you to build modern web applications. In other words, you code up your website using Python and then behind the scenes, fast HTML takes the Python code and translates it into the HTML and CSS that all web browsers understand. And one of the key upsides of fast HTML is that you can combine it with most front-end frameworks. For example, I'm using Tailwind CSS, which is a CSS framework, which has these custom classes that help make your website look more modern. Here are some of the features of Tailwind CSS. So, we've got responsive design, filters, dark mode, CSS variables out of the box, this easy to use color palette, and so much more. While using Tailwind CSS is a lot easier than base CSS, it's still pretty low-level. So, there are actually a lot of UI component libraries built on top of Tailwind. For example, the one that I'm using right now is called Daisy UI. This adds another layer of abstraction on top of Tailwind. The key benefit here is that it has a bunch of components you can grab out of the box, which is what we're seeing here. They have a bunch of color templates that you can use out of the box that actually look good. So, you don't have to be a designer to make a website that looks good. This is great for someone like me. And they also have this cool theme generator. So, I generated a theme here, but you can start with one of their base themes and then you can make some customization to it. You can pick your color palette, how the boxes and fields and cards look, and then you can get a preview of various components here. Another upside of fast HTML is that you can use it to build both your front end and your back end. This works a lot like fast API. Basically, all you need to do is to add this route decorator to your regular Python functions and you instantly turn them into web pages or end points. When it comes to the AI part of my AI apps, I mainly use OpenAI's API. This comes, of course, with all of their models, but it also comes with handy things such as out of the box tool use. So, you can just define what tools you want to give the LLM. You can add things like web search all through the API and you don't have to set up the tool calling yourself. They also have things like conversation state, background mode, streaming, structured outputs, which I don't see here. A lot of handy stuff comes out of the box with their API. And for storage, I use SQL light, which is a lightweight database that lives in the directory of your application. And it's actually a standard module in Python via the SQLite 3 module. And although SQLite typically won't scale beyond 10,000 users or so, if I'm lucky enough for this to become a problem, I can always do a database migration. But until then, I'm going to stick with SQLite to keep things simple, fast, and cheap. We talked about the programming language, the front end, and the back end. Now, let's talk about my development tools. If you're not using AI to help you code, you are moving 5 to 10 times slower than you would be otherwise. While there are many ways to do this today, I use Cursor for the majority of my application development. And I'm sure you've heard about Cursor. It's just this IDE that has AI built into the user experience. And this has been my go-to IDE for the past 12 months. For managing Python projects and dependencies, I use UV. Not only is UV super fast, it also automatically keeps track of your project dependencies, which has made my life much easier when pushing code to production. And finally, for quickly exploring and iterating on system design choices, I like to use Jupyter notebooks. Although you can run notebooks in cursor and most common idees, I prefer the user interface of Jupyter Lab, which is a Python library that you can easily install. And typically what I'll do is just launch Jupiter lab from my IDE in the browser and just have these two development environments. So I'll be writing code in cursor and then if I want to do a quick experiment, I'll jump over to Jupyter Lab in my browser do like a quick investigate some class that I don't understand or try to implement something real quick or try to debug some issue. Having that area separate from the development environment is actually super helpful. Now moving on to deployment. For actually deploying apps into production, I use Railway, which is an infrastructure platform that makes it super easy to get your apps in the cloud. And one super nice thing about Railway is that it makes it easy to deploy your apps directly from a GitHub repository. And so this makes my workflow super smooth. I simply update my code locally, then push them to a private Git repository like this one here, and then automatically my changes will get pushed to production. So far, we've covered all of my developer tools from the language, the front end, the backend, development environment, and deployment. But there are a few other tools that are essential to my workflow. The first is Canva. Before writing any code, I do the two following things in Canva. The first is I create a brand template. So, I put together things like a color palette, come up with various designs for logos, and I pick fonts. The second thing I do is create mockups for what I want the app to actually look like. Here we have a mockup I created recently for a new app that I'm working on called Ghost. For me, it's much easier to iterate on designs in Canva than trying to do it at the keyboard, especially cuz I'm pretty new to front-end development. And then what I can do from here is export this image, upload it to an LLM via cursor, and it's going to come up with a helpful starting point in actually implementing this front end. For some reason, it didn't make it on my list here, but the next tool I use is Cloudflare. And I use this for registering and managing my domains. The great thing about Cloudflare is that they sell you domains at cost, which a lot of platforms don't. They usually have some kind of markup on it. Also, Cloudflare is such a commonly used service. Whenever I want to connect the domain to the actual application, it's a pretty streamlined process. Next, I'm using Kit to manage the weight list for my new AI app. I was already using this for my personal newsletter. So, it made it a pretty natural choice when it came to trying to capture emails and send out blasts, whether it's updates on the product or just general marketing material. For accepting payments and managing subscriptions, I'm using Stripe. Stripe is probably the most popular payment processor I've seen for SAS founders. Plus, I was already using it for other ventures, so this was also a pretty easy choice for me. Finally, to track how users are interacting with my app, I'm using Google Analytics. This is a free tool, and I've also used this on my past websites. So, it allows me to have the analytics for all my sites in one place. So, that's my stack. If you have any questions about these tools or even suggestions for me, please drop them in the comments below. And as always, thank you so much for your time and thanks for watching.
